preamble: While a direct, unconditioned historical sound change from an alveolar trill /r/ to a voiceless postalveolar affricate /tʃ/ is uncommon across languages, such a transformation can occur as a regular morphophonological alternation within specific linguistic systems, often driven by phonotactic constraints.
phoneticEffects: This shift involves affrication, where a trill becomes an affricate, and palatalization, as the place of articulation moves from alveolar to postalveolar

examples.note: In Dholuo noun pluralization, stem-final /r/ undergoes an alternation to [tʃ] in plural forms. This change serves to repair illicit *[rt] sequences, which are disallowed in the language.

sources: Onyango, J. (2024). Morphophonology of Dholuo noun pluralization. In C. R. Green & S. Lotven (Eds.), The Ghanaian linguistics nexus (pp. 247-278). Language Science Press.
